params.text:
Standard infection control precautions (SICPs)

Standard precautions are the basic measures you apply to everyone, all the time, because you can never be certain who is carrying an infection. They assume that any blood or body fluid may be infectious. UKHSA and the National IPC Manual group them under a small number of headings.

  • Hand hygiene - the single most important action (covered next).
  • Respiratory and cough hygiene - "catch it, bin it, kill it": cover the mouth and nose with a tissue, dispose of it, then clean hands.
  • Personal protective equipment (PPE) - selected according to the task and risk of splash or contact.
  • Safe management of the care environment - routine cleaning and decontamination.
  • Safe management of equipment - clean, disinfect or use single-use items.
  • Safe management of blood and body fluid spillages.
  • Safe management of care linen and waste.
  • Safe handling and disposal of sharps.
  • Occupational safety - vaccination, staying off work when unwell, reporting exposures.

When a person has a specific infection that spreads easily (for example diarrhoea and vomiting, or a multidrug-resistant organism), you add transmission-based precautions on top - such as isolating them in their room and using extra PPE.

params.question: Which of the following are standard infection control precautions that apply to every person you support? (Select all that apply.)

answers.text: Cleaning your hands at the right moments

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes - hand hygiene is the foundation of standard precautions.
Correct - safe sharps management protects you and others from injury and blood-borne viruses.

answers.text: Only wearing PPE when a person has a diagnosed infection

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. PPE is selected by task and risk of contamination for everyone - not only for people with a known infection.

answers.text: Safe management of blood and body fluid spillages

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct - all spillages are treated as potentially infectious and dealt with promptly.
